summary: Muratori evaluates whether WinDBG Preview has improved enough to replace Visual Studio as his everyday debugger. He documents Visual Studio's steady decline since version 6, noting its painfully slow watch window, inability to show out-of-scope variables, and bloated installation process. After testing WinDBG Preview, he concludes it is now viable as a daily debugger, which he considers great news for developers suffering under Visual Studio's deterioration. He provides a detailed list of remaining usability issues — keyboard shortcuts, breakpoint management, register views, and configuration handling — as constructive feedback to Microsoft. The post serves as both a practical review and a plea for Microsoft to invest in polishing WinDBG's usability to make it a definitive Visual Studio replacement.
key_points: Visual Studio's debugger has deteriorated significantly since version 6, with slow watch windows, multi-second project switching, and inability to inspect out-of-scope variables
WinDBG is actually more powerful than Visual Studio's debugger, is much smaller, and can be run by simply copying a directory — no bloated installer required
WinDBG Preview has crossed the threshold into daily usability, but still needs keyboard shortcut improvements, better breakpoint naming, and decoupled UI/target configuration
The gap between WinDBG and everyday use is primarily UX friction — too many manual steps for common operations like re-running executables and setting breakpoints by name
Microsoft distributing WinDBG only through the Windows Store is a missed opportunity; a ZIP-based distribution would prevent the installer bloat that plagued Visual Studio
money_quotes: The debugger in Visual Studio 2017 is a mess.

The watch window takes seconds to update even for simple displays, and it doesn't even show you that it's busy refreshing, so if the values don't change, you don't even know if it's done updating or not!
In many ways, WinDBG is actually the better debugger, even for non-kernel debugging.
Unlike Visual Studio, it doesn't require a huge several-minute bloated installation process. You can just copy the WinDBG directory to a machine, and it will run.
I'm delighted to report that, although it didn't seem like it at first, once I dug in, WinDBG Preview really does appear to be usable as an everyday debugger!
This is great news for everyone who's been chafing under Visual Studio's deteriorating debugger for the past fifteen years.
Committing to such a deployment would ensure that bloated installer creep couldn't happen (like it has for Visual Studio).
I'm already thinking about ditching Visual Studio for good and using WinDBG exclusively.
key_insight: WinDBG Preview has finally crossed the usability threshold for daily debugging, and with modest UX polish it could fully replace Visual Studio's increasingly degraded debugger.
